title: Angled-Leg Sawhorses for Desks
summary: A pair of splayed-leg 2x2 sawhorses with a built-in shelf between the legs — sturdy enough to carry a desktop, useful enough to keep around after.
description:
"Adjustable" in the source name refers to build height, not an in-use adjustment — there's no hardware here for changing height after assembly. What you actually get is a pair of matched sawhorses with their legs splayed at 15° for real stability under a desk-width span, plus a shelf built into the lower half of each leg assembly that most sawhorse plans skip entirely.

Build both sawhorses' leg assemblies side by side and compare them before final assembly — a pair of desk sawhorses only works as a matched set, and any height or angle mismatch between the two is the kind of thing you don't notice until a desktop is rocking on top of them.

tools.note: Recommended for the leg joints; nails and glue also work.

cutList.note: Both ends cut parallel at 15° off square.
Both ends cut not parallel at 15° off square, longest point measurement.
Both ends cut not parallel at 15°, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut eight leg pieces at 25-7/8", four top pieces at 12-3/4" and four cross braces at 12" from the Pine 2x2. Cut four top gussets at 5-7/8", four inside blocks at 3", four horizontal supports at 18-1/2", eight shelf boards at 21-1/2" and four end caps at 23" from the Pine 1x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft Pine 2x2 and four 8 ft Pine 1x3. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the leg pairs
steps.body: Each sawhorse needs two leg pairs, so build four in all. A leg pair is two 25-7/8" leg pieces — both ends cut parallel at 15° off square — splayed under one 12-3/4" 2x2 top piece, whose ends are cut at 15° but not parallel and measured at the longest point. Orient each leg so the 15° cut at the bottom sits flat on the floor and the matching cut at the top meets the underside of the top piece cleanly; if a leg rocks, you have it end for end. Join with 1-1/2" pocket holes and screws, or with nails and glue as the plan allows — note that the only pocket screw in the materials list is 1-1/4", which is short for 1-1/2" stock, so glue every joint whichever way you fasten. Build all four pairs against the same layout marked on your bench and stand them side by side before the glue sets: a matched pair is the entire point of this plan, and a leg pair 1/4" taller than its mate will rock a desktop forever.

steps.title: Attach the inside blocks
steps.body: Glue and nail one 3" 1x3 inside block onto each leg pair, four in all. It sits on the inside of the assembly under the top piece and gives the angled leg joint a flat, square shoulder to bear against — without it you are asking the pocket screws alone to hold a splayed joint that wants to spread every time weight goes on the horse. Fit the four 5-7/8" 1x3 top gussets at the same time, one per leg pair: their ends are cut at 15° not parallel, longest point, which is exactly the angle that lets them lie flat across the leg-to-top joint and lock the splay. The source cuts these gussets but never says to install them, so this is where they belong. Use 1-1/4" brads and glue, and clamp each one until the glue grabs.

steps.title: Attach the horizontal supports
steps.body: Stand two leg pairs facing each other and join them with two 18-1/2" 1x3 horizontal supports, one on each side, low down where the shelf will sit. There are exactly four supports in the cut list — two per sawhorse, a single tier, not two — so set both at the same height measured from the floor on all four legs, and clamp a spacer block under each while you fasten so it cannot creep. With 18-1/2" between the leg assemblies and a 1-1/2" leg at each end, each sawhorse finishes 21-1/2" long overall, which is exactly the length of the shelf boards; that number is your check that the supports went on square. Glue and nail with 1-1/4" brads. The four 12" 2x2 cross braces also belong somewhere in this assembly, two per sawhorse, but the source never places them — dry-fit them against the splayed legs, pick the position that best stiffens the horse against racking, and fasten only once you are satisfied. Then set both sawhorses side by side and compare heights and leg angles; this is the last easy moment to catch a mismatch.

steps.title: Add the shelf boards
steps.body: Lay four 21-1/2" 1x3 shelf boards across the horizontal supports on each sawhorse, running the full 21-1/2" length of the assembly so their ends finish flush with the outside of the legs. Four boards at 2-1/2" wide give 10" of shelf across the span between the legs, so they will not fill it edge to edge — spread them evenly with an offcut as a spacer rather than pushing them all to one side and leaving a gap you will drop tools through. Glue and nail each board into both supports with 1-1/4" brads, two per end, keeping the outer boards parallel to the supports so the shelf does not read crooked from across the room.

steps.title: Cap the tops
steps.body: Finish each sawhorse with two 23" 1x3 end caps laid side by side along the top, spanning both 12-3/4" top pieces. At 23" they run 1-1/4" longer than the sawhorse's 21-1/2" body, so centre them and let the overhang fall evenly at each end; side by side the pair gives a 5"-wide bearing surface for a desktop, and a sacrificial 1x3 face is far better to saw into by accident than the 2x2 top piece underneath. The source cuts these four pieces but never places them, so this position is derived from their 23" length and the two-per-sawhorse count — check it against the photo before you nail. Glue and 1-1/4" brads are plenty. Sand the top faces flat and ease every edge so nothing catches a workpiece as you slide it across.

images.alt: A pair of matched wood sawhorses with splayed legs and a built-in lower shelf
